It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{Sōja shrines}} {{draft categories| }} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Aki Province]] [[Category:Azekura-zukuri]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Take Shrine]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[:en:Inaba|Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Take Shrine]] {{Commons category|Take-jinja}} [[ja:多家神社]] [[no:Take Shrine-helligdommen]] {{wikidata link|Q11430665}} [[sn:2]] [[sn:3]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Jimmu]],

all the deities of its province | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | architecture_style = [[Azekura-zukuri]] }} '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{Sōja shrines}} {{draft categories| }} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Aki Province]] [[Category:Azekura-zukuri]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Take Shrine]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki]] [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Sōja shrine|Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[:en:Inaba|Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Take Shrine]] {{Commons category|Take-jinja}} [[ja:多家神社]] [[no:Take Shrine-helligdommen]] {{wikidata link|Q11430665}} [[sn:2]] [[sn:3]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Jimmu]],

[[Okuninushi]],
all the deities of its province | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | architecture_style = [[Azekura-zukuri]] }} '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{Sōja shrines}} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Aki Province]] [[Category:Azekura-zukuri]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Take Shrine]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki]] [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Sōja shrine|Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[:en:Inaba|Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Take Shrine]] {{Commons category|Take-jinja}} [[ja:多家神社]] [[no:Take Shrine-helligdommen]] {{wikidata link|Q11430665}} [[sn:2]] [[sn:3]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Jimmu]],

all the deities of its province | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | architecture_style = [[Azekura-zukuri]] }} '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Aki Province]] [[Category:Azekura-zukuri]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Take Shrine]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki]] [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Sōja shrine|Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[:en:Inaba|Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Take Shrine]] {{Commons category|Take-jinja}} [[ja:多家神社]] [[no:Take Shrine-helligdommen]] {{wikidata link|Q11430665}} [[sn:2]] [[sn:3]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Jimmu]],

[[Okuninushi]],
all the deities of its province | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | architecture_style = [[Azekura-zukuri]] }} '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Aki Province]] [[Category:Azekura-zukuri]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Take Shrine]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki]] [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Sōja shrine|Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[:en:Inaba|Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Take Shrine]] {{Commons category|Take-jinja}} [[ja:多家神社]] [[no:Take Shrine-helligdommen]] {{wikidata link|Q11430665}} [[sn:2]] [[sn:3]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Jimmu]],

The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Jimmu]],
{{ill|Akitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|安芸津彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} (founder of [[Aki Province]]),
[[Empress Jingu]],
[[Emperor Ojin]],
[[Okuninushi]],
all the deities of its province | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | architecture_style = [[Azekura-zukuri]] }} '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Aki Province]] [[Category:Azekura-zukuri]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Take Shrine]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Take-jinja]][[en:Take Shrine]][[ja:多家神社]][[no:Take-helligdommen]][[simple:Take Shrine]] [[co:commons:Category:Take-jinja]][[en:Take Shrine]][[ja:多家神社]][[no:Take-helligdommen]][[simple:Take Shrine]] [[sn:commons:Category:Take-jinja]][[en:Take Shrine]][[ja:多家神社]][[no:Take-helligdommen]][[simple:Take Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430665]] Wikidata name: Take-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg | deity = {{ill|Jimmu|WD=Q200188}} | type = [[Sōja shrine|Sōja shrine]] | architecture_style = [[:en:Azekura-zukuri|Azekura-zukuri]] | prefecture= {{ill|Fuchu|WD=Q1204737}} | coordinates= {{Coord|34.395906|N|132.510253|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[:en:Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[:en:Aki Province|Aki]] [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Sōja shrine|Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[:en:Hiroshima Castle|Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[:en:Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[:en:Inaba|Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[en:Take Shrine]] {{Commons category|Take-jinja}} [[ja:多家神社]] [[no:Take Shrine-helligdommen]] {{wikidata link|Q11430665}} [[sn:2]] [[sn:3]] [[sn:4]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Jimmu]],

This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Hiroshima}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | deity = [[Emperor Jimmu]],
{{ill|Akitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|安芸津彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} (founder of [[Aki Province]]),
[[Empress Jingu]],
[[Emperor Ojin]],
[[Okuninushi]],
all the deities of its province | type = [[Sōja (Shinto)|Sōja shrine]] | architecture_style = [[Azekura-zukuri]] }} '''Take Shrine''' (多家神社) is a [[Sōja shrine]] in [[Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|Fuchu]], [[Aki District, Hiroshima]]. It is a [[Sōja shrine]] so it enshrines all the [[kami]] of the shrines in [[Aki Province|Aki]] [[Provinces of Japan|Province]]. It is located on the site of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. It was mentioned in 927 in the [[Engishiki]] and highly Ranked as a [[Myojin Taisha]]..{{Cite web |url=https://aki-fuchu.jp/english/introduction_e_right.htm |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=aki-fuchu.jp}} There used to be an earlier [[Soja Shrine]] before it was moved here This shrine is one of the "Three Great Shrines of Aki Province", along with [[Itsukushima Shrine]] and [[Hayatani Shrine]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LPq-lhGqzgEC&q=Hayatani+Shrine |title=Hiroshima |date=1986 |publisher=Hiroshima City |language=en}} According to shrine tradition, the original Take Shrine was established on the site of the [[Takemikazuchi]] Palace (mentioned in the Kojiki, an ancient Japanese text) or Enomiya (mentioned in the Nihon Shoki, another ancient text), where Emperor Jinmu stayed for seven years during his eastern expedition.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=shFDAQAAIAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=The Japan Daily Mail |date=1899 |language=en}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VB9CAQAAMAAJ&q=%22take+shrine%22+hiroshima |title=Japan Weekly Mail |date=1899 |language=en}} However the status of the shrine gradually declined over time from being one of the three great shrines, and its location was lost during the [[Edo period]].{{Cite web |title=Take Shrine Treasure House - Hiroshimatatemonogatari |url=https://www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp/soshiki_file/tatemonogatari/en/page062.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.pref.hiroshima.lg.jp}} Its treasure house is one of the few remaining buildings of [[Hiroshima Castle]]. The Take Shrine's treasure house managed to survive a fire in 1915.{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Cultural Encyclopedia - Take Shrine's Treasure House in the Azekura Style - |url=http://www.hiroshima-bunka.jp/english/detail/021.html |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=www.hiroshima-bunka.jp}} Ichinomiya and Soja are not the same thing but were sometimes combined{{Cite web |date=2023-11-21 |title=Ichinomiya / Sōja {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |access-date=2023-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231121195632/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8841 |archive-date=2023-11-21 }} {{nihongo|Sōja|総社|}} is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] where the ''[[kami]]'' of a region are grouped together into a single sanctuary. This "region" may refer to a ''[[shōen]]'', village or geographic area, but is more generally referred to a whole [[Provinces of Japan|province]]. The term is also occasionally called "sōsha". The ''sōja'' are usually located near the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] established in the [[Nara period]] under then ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, and can either be a newly created shrine, or a designation for an existing shrine. The "sōja" can also be the "[[ichinomiya]]" of the province, which themselves are of great ritual importance.{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=Jean |title=Shinto:At the Fountain-head of Japan |date=2011 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-0-415-59348-9 |page=105}} Whenever a new [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] was appointed by the central government to govern a province, it was necessary for him to visit all of the sanctuaries of his province in order to complete the rites necessary for ceremonial inauguration. Grouping the ''kami'' into one location near the capital of the province greatly facilitated this duty,{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |date=2016 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1138979079}} The first mention of "sōja" appeared in the [[Heian period]], in the diary of Taira no Tokinori, dated March 9, 1099 in reference to the province of [[Inaba]].{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0190621711}} ==Gallery== File:Take Shrine 1941.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine 1941 File:Take-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-1 File:Take-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Haiden-2 File:Take-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Honden File:Take-jinja, houzou-1.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, houzou-2.jpg|alt=|Treasure House File:Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Kifune-jinja File:Take-jinja, shamusho.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Shamusho File:Take-jinja, Ten-jinja.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Ten-jinja File:Take-jinja, torii-1.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-1 File:Take-jinja, torii-2.jpg|alt=|Take-jinja, Torii-2 File:多家神社 Take shrine - panoramio.jpg|alt=|Take Shrine Panoramio File:多家神社01.JPG|alt= File:多家神社02.JPG|alt= File:多家神社03.JPG|alt= File:多家神社04.JPG|alt= File:多家神社05.JPG|alt= ==References== {{reflist}} ]] {{shinto shrines}} {{Sōja shrines}} [[sn:Take Shrine]] [[en:Take Shrine]] [[ja:Take Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Accidentally overwritten pages]] [[Category:Aki Province]] [[Category:Azekura-zukuri]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Aki Province]] [[Category:Sōja shrines]] [[Category:Take Shrine]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]
